marks.label: Five tapering arms
marks.description: The classic orange star: five stout arms tapering to points. Turns up almost anywhere, often near mussel beds.

marks.label: Pale spine line down each arm
marks.description: A single pale line of low, knobbly spines runs down the middle of each arm - no big spike rows like the spiny starfish.

perMark.label: Five tapering arms
perMark.note: The circle is extremely large and encompasses the entire starfish rather than highlighting the overall body shape cleanly.

perMark.label: Pale spine line down each arm
perMark.note: The circle is well placed on one of the arms, clearly showing the pale line of spines running down the center.

validation.summary: The photo is excellent and clearly shows the diagnostic features of the Common Starfish. Circle 1 should be resized or repositioned to more elegantly highlight the five tapering arms without swallowing the entire subject.

marks.label: Five short stubby arms
marks.description: Five short, fat arms rather than long points - giving a plump, cushiony feel.

marks.label: Pentagon outline
marks.description: The whole animal is a small (to 5 cm) greenish, knobbly pentagon, not a pointed star. Found under stones on the lower shore.

perMark.label: Five short stubby arms
perMark.note: The inner circle clearly encompasses the central body and the short, stubby arms radiating from it.

perMark.label: Pentagon outline
perMark.note: The outer circle perfectly frames the overall pentagonal shape of the cushion star.

validation.summary: The concentric circles are well placed to show both the central body with its short arms and the overall pentagonal outline of the cushion star.

marks.label: Bright silver lateral stripe
marks.description: A very bright, solid silver stripe runs the whole length of the flank, the sand smelt's most conspicuous feature.

marks.label: Translucent greenish body
marks.description: A slender, semi-transparent body with a greenish tinge above the silver stripe; a small (to ~15 cm), near-surface schooler.

marks.label: Two small dorsal fins
marks.description: Two small, separate dorsal fins; the first is tiny and spiny; both are set well back on the body.

perMark.label: Bright silver lateral stripe
perMark.note: The circle is placed too far back and is too small, but it does clip the silver stripe.

perMark.label: Translucent greenish body
perMark.note: The circle is extremely large and covers the entire front half of the fish, including the silver stripe and belly.

perMark.label: Two small dorsal fins
perMark.note: The circle is placed off the fish entirely, floating in the background above the tail region.

validation.summary: The circles are poorly sized and misaligned. Circle 1 should be centered on the bright silver stripe, Circle 2 should be smaller and focused on the translucent greenish back, and Circle 3 must be moved onto the actual dorsal fins.

marks.label: Four gonad rings
marks.description: The four pale horseshoe loops on top of the bell are the gonads. They are the single most reliable moon-jelly tell; no other UK jelly shows this four-ring clover.

marks.label: Four frilly oral arms
marks.description: Four ribbon-like mouth-arms hang from the centre of the bell. They are short and frilly, not the long stinging tentacles of a lion's mane.

marks.label: Short tentacle fringe
marks.description: Only a fine fringe of very short tentacles rings the bell margin (vs the dense trailing mane of Cyanea). Harmless to touch.

perMark.label: Four gonad rings
perMark.note: The circle is well placed over the pale horseshoe-shaped gonads, though they are slightly faint in this specimen.

perMark.label: Four frilly oral arms
perMark.note: The circle perfectly highlights the translucent, ribbon-like oral arms hanging down.

perMark.label: Short tentacle fringe
perMark.note: The fine, hair-like fringe along the bell margin is exceptionally clear and well-targeted.

validation.summary: The annotations are highly accurate and clearly highlight the key diagnostic features of the moon jellyfish. No changes are needed.

marks.label: Tall first dorsal (male)
marks.description: Breeding males raise a very tall, sail-like first dorsal fin (females and resting fish keep it folded flat).

marks.label: Flat-bottomed, sits on sand
marks.description: A flattened body that lies pressed to the sand, with eyes set high on top of the head — a scooter, not a free-swimmer.

marks.label: Blue stripes & spots (male)
marks.description: Electric-blue wavy stripes and spots across the head and body of the male; females are plain mottled brown.

perMark.label: Tall first dorsal (male)
perMark.note: The first dorsal fin is folded flat and barely visible, not tall or sail-like in this specimen.

perMark.label: Flat-bottomed, sits on sand
perMark.note: The circle is excessively large and centered mostly on the empty sand behind the fish.

perMark.label: Blue stripes & spots (male)
perMark.note: The circle perfectly highlights the bright blue and yellow patterns on the cheek and gill cover.

validation.summary: The first dorsal fin is folded down so mark 1 cannot show a tall fin, but the other features are visible. Shrink and center mark 2 on the flat body resting on the sand, and adjust mark 1 to point to the eyes on top of the head instead.

marks.label: Flat-headed, sits on sand
marks.description: A small flat-headed dragonet that lies pressed to sandy-muddy bottoms with eyes set high on top of the head. Smaller than the common dragonet.

marks.label: Cream-and-brown spotty camouflage
marks.description: Finely spotted and reticulated (net-patterned) in cream and brown for camouflage on sand, hence the spotted dragonet.

marks.label: Modest first dorsal (male)
marks.description: The male raises a first dorsal fin in display, but it is lower and less filamentous than the tall sail of the common dragonet.

perMark.label: Flat-headed, sits on sand
perMark.note: The circle correctly highlights the flat head and high-set eyes of the dragonet.

perMark.label: Cream-and-brown spotty camouflage
perMark.note: The circle is placed over the dorsal fin area instead of the spotted body pattern.

perMark.label: Modest first dorsal (male)
perMark.note: The circle is placed on a huge area covering the mid-body and pectoral fin rather than the first dorsal fin.

validation.summary: The circles for marks 2 and 3 are swapped and misaligned. Circle 2 should be moved to the spotted body flank, and Circle 3 should be resized and moved to the first dorsal fin.

marks.label: Sharp cone, straight sides
marks.description: A glossy, sharply conical shell with straight sides and a flat base, like a smooth spinning top. Pinkish flecks over 10-12 whorls.

marks.label: Pointed apex, no navel
marks.description: Comes to a fine point at the top, and (unlike the flat top shell) has no open navel hole on the underside.

perMark.label: Sharp cone, straight sides
perMark.note: The large circle perfectly highlights the straight, flat-sided conical whorls with pinkish flecks.

perMark.label: Pointed apex, no navel
perMark.note: The circle is well-placed on the pointed apex, though the lack of an open navel on the underside cannot be seen from this angle.

validation.summary: The annotations are highly accurate and clearly demonstrate the diagnostic straight-sided cone shape and pointed apex of the shell. No changes are needed.

marks.label: Pie-crust shell edge
marks.description: The front margin of the broad oval shell is crimped like the edge of a pie - unmistakable once you've seen it.

marks.label: Pie-crust shell edge
marks.description: The front edge of the broad reddish shell is crimped like the crust of a pie - the classic edible (brown) crab mark.

marks.label: Broad oval shell
marks.description: A wide, smooth oval shell (reddish-brown in life) with big black-tipped pincers. The biggest crab you'll meet on the shore.

marks.label: Black-tipped claws
marks.description: Both heavy pincers end in black tips.

perMark.label: Pie-crust shell edge
perMark.note: The circle is extremely large and covers the left side of the carapace rather than focusing on the crimped edge.

perMark.label: Pie-crust shell edge
perMark.note: The circle is oversized and covers the right side of the carapace instead of highlighting the crimped margin.

perMark.label: Broad oval shell
perMark.note: The large central circle correctly encompasses the broad oval carapace of the crab.

perMark.label: Black-tipped claws
perMark.note: The circle is well placed on the black tip of the left claw.

validation.summary: The circles for the pie-crust shell edge (1 and 2) are far too large and overlap heavily with the main carapace circle. They should be resized and shifted to target the crimped outer margins of the shell.

marks.label: Five teeth each side
marks.description: Count five sharp teeth along each side of the shell, behind the eyes (plus three blunt bumps between the eyes). The classic shore-crab tell.

marks.label: Pointed walking legs
marks.description: The legs end in simple points, never flattened into swimming paddles - that rules out the velvet and harbour crabs.

perMark.label: Five teeth each side
perMark.note: The circle is well centered on the carapace showing the five anterolateral teeth clearly on the left side.

perMark.label: Pointed walking legs
perMark.note: The circle clearly highlights the pointed tips of the rear walking legs, showing they are not swimming paddles.

validation.summary: The annotations are highly accurate and clearly highlight the diagnostic features of the shore crab. No changes are needed.

marks.label: Thick upper lip
marks.description: The upper lip is very thick (nearly as deep as the eye diameter) and warty-looking — the give-away vs thin-lipped and golden-grey mullet.

marks.label: Grey, striped flanks
marks.description: Plain grey-silver body with faint horizontal dark streaks along each scale row; a robust, deep-bodied mullet.

marks.label: Two separated dorsal fins
marks.description: A short spiny first dorsal followed by a longer soft second fin, well separated as in all grey mullet.

perMark.label: Thick upper lip
perMark.note: The circle is well placed on the thick, pale upper lip.

perMark.label: Grey, striped flanks
perMark.note: The circle is far too large, though it does encompass the striped flanks.

perMark.label: Two separated dorsal fins
perMark.note: The circle is massive and centered on the empty water above the fish, missing the dorsal fins entirely.

validation.summary: The thick lip is correctly marked, but the circles for the flanks and dorsal fins are far too large and misaligned. Shrink circle 2 to focus on the mid-body stripes, and reposition a smaller circle 3 to target the dorsal fins.

marks.label: Radiating compass marks
marks.description: Dark brown V-shapes radiate from the centre of the bell like the points of a compass - the single best tell for this species.

marks.label: Dark central ring
marks.description: A small dark ring sits at the very top of the bell, where all the V-marks meet.

marks.label: Long trailing tentacles
marks.description: Twenty-four long, fine tentacles trail from the bell margin, plus four frilly mouth-arms. It stings.

perMark.label: Radiating compass marks
perMark.note: The large circle perfectly encompasses the radiating brown V-shaped markings on the bell.

perMark.label: Dark central ring
perMark.note: The circle is perfectly centered on the distinct dark ring at the apex of the bell.

perMark.label: Long trailing tentacles
perMark.note: The circle correctly highlights the frilly mouth-arms and trailing tentacles extending from the bell.

validation.summary: The annotations are exceptionally well-placed and clearly highlight all three diagnostic features of the compass jellyfish.

marks.label: Long scaleless grey body
marks.description: A heavy, scaleless, grey-brown eel body (the true eel is yellow-brown and freshwater). Hides in wreck and rock holes by day.

marks.label: Dorsal fin starts forward
marks.description: The long dorsal fin begins just behind the pectoral fin and runs to the tail — much further forward than in the common eel.

marks.label: Big head, wide gape
marks.description: A large head with a wide mouth and stout jaws; it does have pectoral fins (a moray has none).

perMark.label: Long scaleless grey body
perMark.note: The large circle correctly encompasses a major portion of the long, grey, scaleless body.

perMark.label: Dorsal fin starts forward
perMark.note: The circle is placed too far back on the body and is too small to clearly highlight the start of the dorsal fin near the pectoral fin.

perMark.label: Big head, wide gape
perMark.note: The circle is too large and encompasses the entire front half of the eel rather than focusing specifically on the head and mouth.

validation.summary: The photo is excellent and shows the entire eel clearly. However, circle 2 needs to be moved forward to target the start of the dorsal fin, and circle 3 should be shrunk to focus specifically on the head and mouth.

marks.label: Dark spot on upper tail base
marks.description: A bold black spot at the TOP of the tail base, the goldsinny's signature mark.

marks.label: Dark spot at front of dorsal
marks.description: A second dark blotch at the very front of the dorsal fin. The two black spots together clinch it.

marks.label: Small reddish-brown body
marks.description: Our smallest common wrasse (to ~15 cm), slender and reddish-brown, hugging rocky crevices.

perMark.label: Dark spot on upper tail base
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the rocky background above the fish, completely missing the very obvious black tail spot.

perMark.label: Dark spot at front of dorsal
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the rocky background above the head of the fish.

perMark.label: Small reddish-brown body
perMark.note: The circle is extremely large and covers the front half of the fish, but is offset too high into the rocks.

validation.summary: The photo itself is excellent and clearly shows all diagnostic features, but the circles are completely misaligned. Reposition circle 1 onto the prominent black tail spot, circle 2 onto the front of the dorsal fin, and resize circle 3 to neatly encompass the fish's body.

marks.label: Reddish-brown bell
marks.description: A broad, domed bell in warm reddish-brown - the UK's largest jelly. Cooler-coloured blue jelly (C. lamarckii) is the smaller lookalike.

marks.label: Dense mane of tentacles
marks.description: Hundreds of long, fine tentacles hang in a thick 'mane' beneath the bell (vs the short fringe of a moon jelly). Painful sting.

marks.label: Orange domed bell
marks.description: A solid orange-tan domed bell.

marks.label: Mane of fine tentacles
marks.description: A dense trailing mass of many fine, hair-like tentacles - the 'lion's mane'.

perMark.label: Orange domed bell
perMark.note: The circle perfectly encompasses the yellow-orange domed bell of the jellyfish.

perMark.label: Mane of fine tentacles
perMark.note: The circle is well placed over the dense mass of trailing fine tentacles.

validation.summary: The annotations are highly accurate and clearly highlight the orange domed bell and the dense mane of tentacles. No changes are needed.

marks.label: Blue-purple bell
marks.description: Vivid blue to purple, the colour deepening towards the centre of the bell. Like a small lion's mane but cool-coloured, not reddish-brown.

marks.label: Radiating canals
marks.description: Dark lines radiate from the centre, with a mass of fine trailing tentacles beneath. Mild sting.

marks.label: Blue-tinted bell
marks.description: A domed bell with a distinctive blue-violet tint and radial markings.

marks.label: Fine trailing tentacles
marks.description: A fringe of many fine tentacles; smaller and bluer than the orange lion's mane.

perMark.label: Blue-purple bell
perMark.note: The circle is extremely large and covers almost the entire body of the jellyfish rather than focusing on the bell.

perMark.label: Radiating canals
perMark.note: The circle is oversized and overlaps with other marks, obscuring the specific radiating lines.

perMark.label: Blue-tinted bell
perMark.note: This circle is also massive and nearly identical in placement to the other bell marks.

perMark.label: Fine trailing tentacles
perMark.note: The circle is too large and encompasses the bell as well as the tentacles.

validation.summary: All four circles are extremely oversized and stacked on top of each other, covering the entire jellyfish. They need to be resized and repositioned to target their specific diagnostic features.

marks.label: Two separate dorsal fins
marks.description: A spiny first dorsal fin and a longer soft second dorsal fin, clearly separated — a perch-family hallmark.

marks.label: Silver-grey flanks, dark back
marks.description: Plain silver-grey flanks scaling up to a darker back; no bars or obvious markings in adults.

marks.label: Two dorsal fins (front spiny)
marks.description: Two separate dorsal fins; the front one is spiny. Clean silver body with no spots.

marks.label: Spiny gill cover
marks.description: Two flat spines on the rear edge of the gill cover (preopercle) — a prick-check confirms it quickly.

marks.label: Spiny gill cover
marks.description: A sharp spine on the rear edge of the gill cover (operculum) - mind it when handling.

marks.label: Pointed head, large mouth
marks.description: Pointed head with a large terminal mouth (vs the blunt, thick-lipped grey mullet).

perMark.label: Two separate dorsal fins
perMark.note: The circle is extremely large and centered on the mid-body rather than the dorsal fins.

perMark.label: Silver-grey flanks, dark back
perMark.note: The circle is too large, but it does encompass the silver flanks and darker back.

perMark.label: Two dorsal fins (front spiny)
perMark.note: The circle is placed too far back, mostly highlighting the second dorsal fin and caudal peduncle.

perMark.label: Spiny gill cover
perMark.note: The circle is correctly centered on the preopercle, though the spines themselves are hard to resolve.

perMark.label: Spiny gill cover
perMark.note: The circle is well-placed on the operculum edge, but the fine spine is difficult to see in this lighting.

perMark.label: Pointed head, large mouth
perMark.note: The circle is excessively large, though it does contain the head and mouth.

validation.summary: Several circles are far too large or misaligned, particularly circle 1 and circle 3 which fail to target the dorsal fins properly. Resizing and shifting the markers to focus tightly on their respective anatomical features will greatly improve clarity.

marks.label: Eight arms, no fins
marks.description: Soft, bulbous body and eight long arms, no fins. Orange-brown; our commonest octopus, on rocky and shelly bottoms.

perMark.label: Eight arms, no fins
perMark.note: The circle is centered on the water and only captures a few of the arms, missing the main body of the octopus.

validation.summary: The circle needs to be resized and shifted down and to the left to fully encompass the octopus body and its arms rather than focusing on the empty water.

marks.label: Long chin barbel
marks.description: A long single whisker under the chin, distinctly longer than bib's; the most reliable cod feature.

marks.label: Pale lateral line
marks.description: A distinct off-white stripe curves along the flank, easiest to see against the mottled back. No sharp kink.

marks.label: Mottled olive-brown back
marks.description: Peppered, mottled camouflage on a green-brown base; cleaner-coloured pollack and saithe lack this speckling.

perMark.label: Long chin barbel
perMark.note: The circle is placed in front of the snout on the dark background, completely missing the chin barbel.

perMark.label: Pale lateral line
perMark.note: The circle is far too large, encompassing almost the entire body of the fish rather than highlighting the lateral line.

perMark.label: Mottled olive-brown back
perMark.note: The circle is oversized and overlaps with circle 2, failing to isolate the dorsal patterning.

validation.summary: The circles are poorly sized and placed, with circle 1 missing the chin entirely and circles 2 and 3 being far too large. Shrink and reposition the circles to target the specific anatomical features on this juvenile cod.

marks.label: Pale band on first dorsal fin
marks.description: A pale orange/yellow band runs along the top edge of the first dorsal fin - the rock goby's tell.

marks.label: Robust dark body
marks.description: A chunky, dark, mottled goby (to ~12 cm), much bigger and darker than the little sand/common gobies. Rocky shores.

marks.label: Pale-edged first dorsal
marks.description: A pale or orange band along the top edge of the front dorsal fin - the rock goby's clearest mark.

marks.label: Blunt head, thick lips
marks.description: A broad blunt head with thick lips, eyes set high.

marks.label: Two dorsal fins
marks.description: Two separate dorsal fins along the back (a goby trait); body stocky and mottled dark.

marks.label: Blunt head, belly sucker
marks.description: Stocky blunt head; the pelvic fins are fused into a round sucker on the belly (shared by all gobies).

perMark.label: Pale band on first dorsal fin
perMark.note: The large circle is centered on the mid-body and substrate rather than the first dorsal fin.

perMark.label: Robust dark body
perMark.note: The circle is placed over the dorsal fins instead of the main body trunk.

perMark.label: Pale-edged first dorsal
perMark.note: The circle correctly highlights the pale upper edge of the first dorsal fin.

perMark.label: Blunt head, thick lips
perMark.note: The circle is well placed over the blunt head and eye of the goby.

perMark.label: Two dorsal fins
perMark.note: The circle is stacked directly on top of mark 3, only showing the first dorsal fin clearly.

perMark.label: Blunt head, belly sucker
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the head, but the pelvic sucker is underneath the body and not visible.

validation.summary: Several circles are misaligned or stacked directly on top of each other. Reposition circle 1 to the first dorsal fin, circle 2 to the main body, and remove or adjust circle 6 since the belly sucker is hidden from this angle.

marks.label: Dark spot behind pectoral fin
marks.description: The first of the two namesake dark spots sits just behind the pectoral fin.

marks.label: Dark spot at the tail base
marks.description: The second spot sits at the tail base. Two spots plus orange-brown with blue speckles equals two-spotted goby.

marks.label: Hovers in midwater shoals
marks.description: Unlike other gobies it does not perch on the bottom; it hovers in loose midwater groups over kelp and weed.

perMark.label: Dark spot behind pectoral fin
perMark.note: The circle is placed too high on the back, completely missing the dark spot which is visible lower down behind the pectoral fin.

perMark.label: Dark spot at the tail base
perMark.note: The circle is slightly offset to the upper left of the dark spot at the tail base.

perMark.label: Hovers in midwater shoals
perMark.note: The large circle correctly encompasses the fish hovering in the water column above the seabed.

validation.summary: Move circle 1 down to align directly over the dark spot behind the pectoral fin, and slightly adjust circle 2 to center it on the tail spot.

marks.label: Long spindly legs
marks.description: Long, thin, spider-like legs - far longer relative to the body than any of the true crabs here.

marks.label: Teardrop shell, pointed snout
marks.description: The shell is teardrop-shaped, narrowing to a forked point at the front, and is often coated in seaweed and hydroids.

marks.label: Triangular pear-shaped shell
marks.description: The shell narrows to a point at the front, giving a triangular, pear-shaped outline.

marks.label: Long spindly legs
marks.description: Long, thin spider-like legs, often draped in weed or debris as camouflage.

perMark.label: Long spindly legs
perMark.note: The circle for mark 1 is completely missing from the image.

perMark.label: Teardrop shell, pointed snout
perMark.note: The circle for mark 2 is completely missing from the image.

perMark.label: Triangular pear-shaped shell
perMark.note: The circle is centered on the carapace, but the shell shape is heavily obscured by algae and debris.

perMark.label: Long spindly legs
perMark.note: The circle correctly highlights several of the long, spindly legs extending from the body.

validation.summary: Marks 1 and 2 are missing from the image entirely. The remaining marks should be re-authored to include all four diagnostic features, or a clearer photo should be used where the carapace shape is less obscured by camouflage.

marks.label: Thick fleshy lips
marks.description: Big, rubbery lips for picking shellfish off the rock, a wrasse hallmark, strongest in the chunky ballan.

marks.label: Single long dorsal fin
marks.description: One continuous dorsal fin runs almost the whole back (spiny at the front, soft behind), not split like a bass or gadoid.

marks.label: Stout mottled body
marks.description: A deep, robust green-to-brown body, often pale-spotted and highly variable. Our largest wrasse.

perMark.label: Thick fleshy lips
perMark.note: The circle is placed far to the right of the fish on the rocky background.

perMark.label: Single long dorsal fin
perMark.note: The circle is too large and shifted too high, capturing mostly the rocky overhang.

perMark.label: Stout mottled body
perMark.note: The circle is well centered on the beautifully patterned, robust body of the wrasse.

validation.summary: The circles are misaligned, with mark 1 completely off the fish and mark 2 shifted too high. Reposition mark 1 to the lips and shrink/lower mark 2 to target the dorsal fin.

marks.label: Electric blue head & stripes
marks.description: The breeding male is unmistakable: brilliant blue head and wavy blue stripes streaking back over an orange body.

marks.label: Orange flanks
marks.description: Bright orange flanks under the blue lines (males). A slim, pointed-headed wrasse.

perMark.label: Electric blue head & stripes
perMark.note: The circle is far too large, encompassing almost the entire fish and background instead of focusing on the head.

perMark.label: Orange flanks
perMark.note: The circle is oversized and shifted too far forward, capturing more of the blue midsection than the orange flanks.

validation.summary: The photo beautifully showcases a male Cuckoo wrasse, but the annotation circles are far too large and overlap excessively. Shrink and reposition circle 1 to target the blue head, and shrink circle 2 to focus specifically on the orange flanks.

marks.label: Curved lateral line over pectoral
marks.description: A distinct concave arch in the lateral line just above the pectoral fin, the dab's single most reliable tell against plaice and flounder.

marks.label: Rough sandpaper skin
marks.description: The scales feel rough and sandpapery to the touch; the only common UK flatfish with this texture.

marks.label: Plain sandy-brown, right-eyed
marks.description: Both eyes on the right (upper) side; a plain sandy-brown back with no bright orange spots, plainer than plaice.

perMark.label: Curved lateral line over pectoral
perMark.note: The circle is placed far too back on the body, completely missing the pectoral fin and the curved lateral line above it.

perMark.label: Rough sandpaper skin
perMark.note: The circle is on the edge of the dorsal fin rather than the main body skin, and texture is difficult to appreciate visually.

perMark.label: Plain sandy-brown, right-eyed
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the rear half of the body, missing the eyes entirely, though it does show the sandy coloration.

validation.summary: The circles are misaligned and clustered near the tail. Move circle 1 forward to the curved lateral line above the pectoral fin, move circle 3 to highlight the eyes and head region, and adjust circle 2 to clearly target the main body scales.

marks.label: Flattened swimming paddles
marks.description: The last pair of legs end in flat, paddle-shaped segments (tips often tinged violet) for swimming - shared with the velvet crab, absent in the shore crab.

marks.label: Five side teeth
marks.description: Five teeth down each side of the shell behind the eyes; reddish overall. A small swimming crab of muddy sand.

marks.label: Paddle back legs
marks.description: The last pair of legs are flattened into swimming paddles - it is a swimming crab.

marks.label: Broad reddish shell
marks.description: A broad reddish-orange shell with the eyes set at the front.

perMark.label: Flattened swimming paddles
perMark.note: Clearly shows the flattened, violet-tinged swimming paddle on the left side of the image.

perMark.label: Five side teeth
perMark.note: The circle is well-placed on the anterolateral teeth along the side of the carapace.

perMark.label: Paddle back legs
perMark.note: Clearly highlights the other flattened swimming paddle on the right side of the image.

perMark.label: Broad reddish shell
perMark.note: The circle is extremely large and includes a lot of the sandy background above the crab.

validation.summary: The annotations are mostly accurate, but circle 4 is far too large and captures too much of the surrounding gravel. Shrink and center circle 4 more tightly on the reddish carapace.

marks.label: No head tentacles
marks.description: The crown is smooth - NO branched tentacles above the eyes (that rules in the shanny vs the tompot blenny).

marks.label: Thick lips, big high-set eyes
marks.description: A blunt, scaleless head with thick lips and large eyes set high and forward - a true blenny.

marks.label: Mottled green-brown body
marks.description: A smooth, scaleless, mottled green-brown body that wedges into crevices and rock pools.

perMark.label: No head tentacles
perMark.note: The circle correctly highlights the smooth crown above the eye, showing the absence of tentacles.

perMark.label: Thick lips, big high-set eyes
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the cheek and gill cover, completely missing the eye and lips.

perMark.label: Mottled green-brown body
perMark.note: The circle is very large and centered on the pectoral fin rather than the main mottled body flank.

validation.summary: Move circle 2 up and forward to cover the eye and thick lips. Reduce the size of circle 3 and shift it slightly to focus on the mottled pattern of the body flank.

marks.label: Torpedo body
marks.description: A slender, streamlined torpedo body - the classic squid shape, schooling in midwater. The main squid of UK fisheries.

marks.label: Long rear fins
marks.description: Two triangular fins at the tail run about 70% of the body length (longer than the European squid's, which reach only about half).

perMark.label: Torpedo body
perMark.note: The circle is well placed on the main streamlined mantle of the squid.

perMark.label: Long rear fins
perMark.note: The circle correctly highlights the long triangular fin running down the side of the mantle.

validation.summary: The annotations are well placed and clearly highlight the key diagnostic features of the Veined Squid. No changes are needed.

marks.label: Triangular tail fins
marks.description: Two triangular fins at the tail, together reaching a bit over half the body length (shorter than the veined squid's ~70%).

marks.label: Big eye + ten arms
marks.description: A large eye and ten arms (two of them long, retractable catching tentacles). Reddish, fast, schooling over the open shelf.

perMark.label: Triangular tail fins
perMark.note: The large triangular fins are clearly visible and well-framed by the circle.

perMark.label: Big eye + ten arms
perMark.note: The large eye and clustered arms are clearly visible inside the circle.

validation.summary: The annotations are well-placed and clearly highlight the diagnostic features of the European Squid. No changes are needed.

marks.label: Long arms, rows of spines
marks.description: Five long, tapering arms carrying three rows of conspicuous spines (each spine ringed blue at its base). Can reach 70 cm across.

marks.label: Conspicuous spines
marks.description: The prominent, evenly-spaced white spines separate it from the smoother common starfish.

perMark.label: Long arms, rows of spines
perMark.note: The circle is extremely large and centered on the underside of the starfish, showing tube feet rather than the dorsal rows of spines.

perMark.label: Conspicuous spines
perMark.note: The circle correctly highlights the prominent spines on the side of the arm.

validation.summary: The starfish is upside down, showing its oral side with tube feet instead of its dorsal side. Circle 1 should be resized and moved to focus on the dorsal spines visible on the upper arm edges, or the photo should be replaced with one showing the dorsal side clearly.

marks.label: Two long chin barbels
marks.description: A pair of long sensory barbels under the chin, used to probe the sand for food, the red mullet's standout feature.

marks.label: Reddish, with stripes/blotches
marks.description: Pink-red flanks marked with reddish-brown stripes and blotches; can flush brighter red. A steeply-sloped forehead.

marks.label: Two separate dorsal fins
marks.description: Two well-separated dorsal fins on a deep-bellied body that grubs along sandy and gravelly bottoms.

perMark.label: Two long chin barbels
perMark.note: The circle is placed too low on the sand, barely catching the tips of the barbels.

perMark.label: Reddish, with stripes/blotches
perMark.note: The circle is well centered on the striped flank and reddish body coloration.

perMark.label: Two separate dorsal fins
perMark.note: The circle only highlights the first dorsal fin, missing the second separate fin.

validation.summary: Move circle 1 up to center on the chin barbels, and expand or shift circle 3 to clearly encompass both separate dorsal fins.

marks.label: Bright red eyes
marks.description: Vivid red eyes are the instant giveaway - no other common UK crab has them.

marks.label: Velvety carapace
marks.description: The shell is coated in fine velvety fur, dull brown. The last legs (not shown here) are flattened swimming paddles.

marks.label: Red eyes
marks.description: Bright red eyes - unmistakable on this otherwise drab crab.

marks.label: Velvety furred shell
marks.description: The shell is covered in fine velvety fur; the last pair of legs are flattened into blue-lined swimming paddles.

perMark.label: Bright red eyes
perMark.note: The circle is extremely large and covers the entire face of the crab rather than focusing on the red eyes.

perMark.label: Velvety carapace
perMark.note: The circle is too large and overlaps with the eye region instead of focusing on the carapace texture.

perMark.label: Red eyes
perMark.note: The circle is placed in the empty water column above the crab's carapace.

perMark.label: Velvety furred shell
perMark.note: The circle is placed in the dark water column completely off the crab.

validation.summary: The current markers are highly redundant and poorly placed, with two circles floating in the empty water column and the others being far too large. The markers should be consolidated and resized to target one red eye and the velvety carapace texture specifically.

marks.label: Pointed spire
marks.description: A short, sharply pointed spire at the top of a thick, solid shell.

marks.label: Short pointed spire
marks.description: A thick, short-spired shell with spiral ridges and an oval mouth drawn out into a little spout. Drills holes in barnacles and mussels.

marks.label: Colour varies with diet
marks.description: The thick body whorl is often boldly banded; colour varies (white to brown/black) with the prey it eats.

marks.label: Colour varies with diet
marks.description: White, grey, banded brown or even orange - the colour follows what it has been eating, so a cluster looks like a mixed bag.

perMark.label: Short pointed spire
perMark.note: The circle is shifted too far to the left, capturing mostly the blurry background instead of centering on the spire.

perMark.label: Colour varies with diet
perMark.note: The circle is shifted too far to the left, overlapping the rock background rather than centering on the banded shell pattern.

validation.summary: The circles are shifted too far to the left, capturing mostly the background. Reposition circle 2 to center on the pointed spire and circle 4 to center on the banded shell pattern.

marks.label: Bulbous, warty body
marks.description: A large bulbous head and eight arms with warty, mottled skin that changes colour in a blink. Dens in rock holes; mostly southern and western in the UK.

perMark.label: Bulbous, warty body
perMark.note: The circle is well-placed over the central body and arms, clearly showing the mottled, textured skin.

validation.summary: The annotation is well-placed and clearly highlights the textured, mottled body of the octopus. No changes are needed.

marks.label: Long thin spiny arms
marks.description: Five long, thread-thin arms fringed with fine spines - far more delicate than a true starfish's arms, and they snap off at a touch.

marks.label: Small disc, dense beds
marks.description: A small round central disc sits at the centre of the arms. Hugely variable in colour; often carpets the seabed in writhing beds like this.

perMark.label: Long thin spiny arms
perMark.note: The large circle perfectly encompasses the radiating spiny arms of the brittlestar.

perMark.label: Small disc, dense beds
perMark.note: The circle is centered on the small central disc, though this is a solitary specimen rather than a dense bed.

validation.summary: The circles are well-placed on the central disc and the spiny arms. The image shows a solitary specimen on a black background rather than a dense bed, but the anatomical features are exceptionally clear.

marks.label: Lives in a borrowed shell
marks.description: Not a true crab: a soft-bodied animal living inside an empty snail shell (a periwinkle when small, a whelk when bigger). The shell is the giveaway.

marks.label: Legs and claws poke out
marks.description: Only the legs and unequal claws show, the right claw the larger - used to block the shell entrance when threatened.

perMark.label: Lives in a borrowed shell
perMark.note: The circle correctly highlights the textured snail shell that the hermit crab is inhabiting.

perMark.label: Legs and claws poke out
perMark.note: The circle is well placed over the prominent legs and claws emerging from the shell opening.

validation.summary: The annotations are well placed and clearly show both the borrowed shell and the emerging legs and claws. No changes are needed.

marks.label: Radiating ridges
marks.description: A low cone of grey shell with ridges radiating from the peak. Steeper-coned high on the shore, flatter lower down.

marks.label: Clamped to bare rock
marks.description: No spire and no pattern - just a plain cone clamped tight to the rock, grazing algae and homing to the same scar.

perMark.label: Radiating ridges
perMark.note: The circle for mark 1 is completely missing from the image.

perMark.label: Clamped to bare rock
perMark.note: The circle perfectly highlights the limpet clamped tightly to its home scar on the bare rock.

validation.summary: The image clearly shows the limpet clamped to the rock, but mark 1 is missing entirely. Add a new circle pointing to the radiating ridges on the shell to complete the annotations.

marks.label: Warty stinging bell
marks.description: The mauve-purple bell is peppered all over with dark stinging warts - unique among the common UK jellies. Glows blue at night.

marks.label: Frilly oral arms
marks.description: Four long, frilly mouth-arms hang from the centre, between the bell and the tentacles.

marks.label: Long trailing tentacles
marks.description: Eight long, slender tentacles trail well below the bell. A notable stinger; fully oceanic, arrives in swarms.

perMark.label: Warty stinging bell
perMark.note: The circle perfectly highlights the distinctive dark stinging warts covering the bell.

perMark.label: Frilly oral arms
perMark.note: The circle is well placed over the long, spotted oral arms hanging from the center.

perMark.label: Long trailing tentacles
perMark.note: The circle correctly captures several of the thin, trailing tentacles extending below the body.

validation.summary: The photo is of excellent quality and all three diagnostic features are clearly visible and perfectly aligned with the markers.

marks.label: Row of dark eye-spots
marks.description: A line of white-ringed black eye-spots runs along the base of the dorsal fin - the unmistakable butterfish mark.

marks.label: Long ribbon body
marks.description: A long, flattened, ribbon-like body, slippery to hold (hence butterfish/gunnel). Drapes over rocks.

marks.label: Dorsal eye-spot row
marks.description: A row of dark, white-ringed eye-spots along the base of the long dorsal fin - unmistakable.

marks.label: Tiny pointed head
marks.description: A small, blunt head on the slender ribbon - not eel-like teeth, just a gentle weed-grazer.

marks.label: Long ribbon body
marks.description: A long, strongly side-flattened ribbon body with one continuous low dorsal fin running its length.

marks.label: Small blunt head
marks.description: Small blunt head; the fish is slippery and eel-like, hence the name butterfish (or gunnel).

perMark.label: Row of dark eye-spots
perMark.note: The circle is extremely large and off-center, though it does encompass one prominent eye-spot.

perMark.label: Long ribbon body
perMark.note: The circle is oversized and mostly covers the sandy substrate rather than focusing on the body.

perMark.label: Dorsal eye-spot row
perMark.note: The marker is placed on the edge of the dorsal fin near an eye-spot but is too small and slightly offset.

perMark.label: Tiny pointed head
perMark.note: The marker is missing entirely from the image.

perMark.label: Long ribbon body
perMark.note: The marker is placed near the pectoral fin area rather than highlighting the main ribbon body.

perMark.label: Small blunt head
perMark.note: The circle is well-placed directly over the head of the fish.

validation.summary: Several markers are missing, duplicated, or poorly sized. The entire set of annotations needs to be re-authored to cleanly target the head, body, and dorsal eye-spots without overlapping giant circles.

marks.label: Bony tubercles along the fin bases
marks.description: A rough run of small bony knobs along the bases of the dorsal and anal fins and the lateral line, rough to the touch unlike the smooth plaice.

marks.label: Pale, scattered spots
marks.description: Any orange spots are pale and scattered, never the vivid, well-defined orange of a plaice.

marks.label: Dull brown estuary flatfish
marks.description: A dull greenish-brown right-eyed flatfish; the one UK flatfish that runs up into brackish estuaries and tidal rivers.

perMark.label: Bony tubercles along the fin bases
perMark.note: The circle is placed over the dorsal fin base but the tiny rough tubercles are very difficult to resolve in this lighting.

perMark.label: Pale, scattered spots
perMark.note: The small circle is placed on a dark mottled patch rather than any distinct pale or faint orange spots.

perMark.label: Dull brown estuary flatfish
perMark.note: The massive circle covers most of the body but is off-center, leaving the head and tail outside the mark.

validation.summary: The circles are poorly sized and misaligned. Circle 3 should be resized to encompass the entire fish to represent the overall body shape and color, while Circle 2 needs to be moved to a region with visible pale spots.

marks.label: Bright orange spots
marks.description: Scattered bright orange-red spots over a smooth brown upper side, the plaice's signature, separating it from dab and flounder.

marks.label: Both eyes on one side
marks.description: A flatfish: both eyes sit on the right (upper) side. The underside it lies on is plain white.

marks.label: Bony ridge behind eyes
marks.description: A short row of 4-7 bony knobs runs from behind the eyes; smooth skin otherwise.

perMark.label: Bright orange spots
perMark.note: The circle is placed near the head instead of the body where the orange spots are clearly visible.

perMark.label: Both eyes on one side
perMark.note: The circle is placed off the fish entirely, floating in the background near the snout.

perMark.label: Bony ridge behind eyes
perMark.note: The circle is close to the eyes but does not clearly highlight the bony ridge structure.

validation.summary: The large unnumbered circle covers the orange spots but is not labeled. Reposition circle 1 to the body spots, circle 2 to the eyes, and adjust circle 3 to target the ridge behind the eyes.

marks.label: Kinked lateral line
marks.description: The dark line down the flank bends sharply upward over the pectoral fin — pollack's most reliable single ID against bib and cod.

marks.label: Projecting lower jaw
marks.description: Lower jaw juts out beyond the upper, giving a slight under-bite silhouette. Cod and bib have an even or upper-jawed bite.

marks.label: No chin barbel
marks.description: Smooth chin — no whisker. This rules out cod and bib at a glance; both gadoids have a clear chin barbel.

perMark.label: Kinked lateral line
perMark.note: The circle correctly encompasses the upward curve of the lateral line above the pectoral fin.

perMark.label: Projecting lower jaw
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the lower chin area rather than the tip of the snout and lower jaw.

perMark.label: No chin barbel
perMark.note: The circle is placed too high, floating in the water column just in front of the snout.

validation.summary: The lateral line circle is well placed, but the circles for the jaw and chin barbel are misaligned and need to be moved to the correct anatomical positions on the fish's head.

marks.label: Straight lateral line
marks.description: The pale line down the flank is nearly straight, with no sharp upward kink over the pectoral fin (that kink is pollack's tell).

marks.label: Level jaw
marks.description: Upper and lower jaws are about equal (pollack's lower jaw juts out). Barbel tiny or absent.

marks.label: Dark greenish back
marks.description: A dark green-brown back over silvery sides, schooling. Also called coalfish or coley.

perMark.label: Straight lateral line
perMark.note: The circle is extremely large and encompasses almost the entire body of the fish rather than focusing on the lateral line.

perMark.label: Level jaw
perMark.note: The circle is well placed over the mouth and snout showing the level jaw structure.

perMark.label: Dark greenish back
perMark.note: The circle is far too large and overlaps heavily with circle 1 instead of targeting just the dorsal region.

validation.summary: The photo is excellent and clearly shows the diagnostic features of the saithe. However, circles 1 and 3 are far too large and need to be resized and repositioned to target their specific features more precisely.

marks.label: Small mottled sandy goby
marks.description: A tiny (to ~6 cm) sandy-mottled goby. Very like the sand goby; common goby favours brackish estuaries and pools.

marks.label: Pelvic-fin sucker
marks.description: Like all gobies, the pelvic fins are fused into a little sucker disc it uses to perch on the bottom.

marks.label: Perched, rounded head
marks.description: Rests propped on the bottom with a blunt rounded head and large eyes set high.

perMark.label: Small mottled sandy goby
perMark.note: The large circle correctly encompasses the entire small mottled goby.

perMark.label: Pelvic-fin sucker
perMark.note: The circle is placed near the pelvic area but is slightly too low and mostly covers the rock surface.

perMark.label: Perched, rounded head
perMark.note: The circle is well centered on the blunt head and high-set eye.

validation.summary: The overall photo and markings are good, but circle 2 should be shifted slightly upward to better target the fused pelvic fin resting on the substrate.

marks.label: Pale sandy body
marks.description: Almost translucent, pale sandy-mottled - matches clean sand. Slightly larger and paler than the common goby.

marks.label: Pelvic-fin sucker
marks.description: Perches on the sand on the fused pelvic-fin sucker shared by all gobies.

marks.label: Rows of small saddles
marks.description: Faint rows of small dark saddles/spots along the back; a row of spots on the first dorsal in males.

perMark.label: Pale sandy body
perMark.note: The circle is extremely large and encompasses almost the entire fish and background rather than highlighting the body coloration specifically.

perMark.label: Pelvic-fin sucker
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the pectoral fin instead of the fused pelvic-fin sucker underneath the body.

perMark.label: Rows of small saddles
perMark.note: The circle is positioned too high, floating partially off the back of the fish into the water column.

validation.summary: The circles are poorly sized and misaligned. Circle 1 needs to be shrunk to focus on the sandy skin texture, Circle 2 must be moved to the pelvic fin area, and Circle 3 should be lowered to sit directly on the dorsal saddles.

marks.label: Solid domed bell
marks.description: A thick, firm, dome-shaped bell (the 'dustbin-lid' jelly) with a violet rim - much more solid than the floppy saucer jellies.

marks.label: Eight frilly arms, no tentacles
marks.description: Eight thick, cauliflower-frilly mouth-arms hang below, and there are NO long trailing tentacles. Mostly harmless.

perMark.label: Solid domed bell
perMark.note: The circle perfectly encompasses the solid, dome-shaped bell with its distinct violet rim.

perMark.label: Eight frilly arms, no tentacles
perMark.note: The circle correctly highlights the thick, cauliflower-like mouth-arms and shows the absence of long trailing tentacles.

validation.summary: The photo is an excellent reference image with perfectly placed circles that clearly show the key diagnostic features of the barrel jellyfish.

marks.label: Wavy green-blue bars
marks.description: Bold, wavy, tiger-stripe bars across a metallic blue-green back, the most eye-catching fish in UK waters.

marks.label: Streamlined forked tail
marks.description: A deeply forked tail and slim, torpedo-shaped body built for sustained fast swimming.

marks.label: Silver-white belly
marks.description: A plain silver-white belly with no bars (the bars only run down the back), sharp contrast with the coloured back.

perMark.label: Wavy green-blue bars
perMark.note: The circle is far too large, encompassing almost the entire body of the fish instead of focusing on the dorsal bars.

perMark.label: Streamlined forked tail
perMark.note: The circle is well placed and clearly highlights the deeply forked tail.

perMark.label: Silver-white belly
perMark.note: The circle is oversized and overlaps heavily with circle 1, making it confusing for a beginner.

validation.summary: The photo itself is excellent, but circles 1 and 3 are far too large and overlap excessively. Shrink and reposition circle 1 to target only the dorsal tiger stripes, and shrink circle 3 to target only the lower silver-white belly.

marks.label: Many small dark spots
marks.description: Densely peppered with small dark brown spots over a sandy-buff body — a small (to ~75 cm) shark, not a fish.

marks.label: Two dorsal fins set far back
marks.description: Both dorsal fins are set well back towards the tail, a shark-shape giveaway; the body is rough like sandpaper.

perMark.label: Many small dark spots
perMark.note: The circle perfectly highlights the dense pattern of small dark spots on the anterior body.

perMark.label: Two dorsal fins set far back
perMark.note: The circle correctly encompasses the posterior region where both dorsal fins are located.

validation.summary: The annotations are highly accurate and clearly highlight the key diagnostic features of the lesser-spotted catshark. No changes are needed.

marks.label: W-shaped pupil
marks.description: The eye has a distinctive wavy, W-shaped pupil - a cuttlefish signature you won't see on a fish.

marks.label: Fin runs the whole body
marks.description: A narrow fin skirts the entire length of the broad, flattened body on both sides (a squid's fins sit only at the tail).

marks.label: Broad mottled body
marks.description: Broad, flattened oval body, mottled brown and able to flash zebra patterns. Our largest cuttlefish; the white cuttlebone often washes up.

perMark.label: W-shaped pupil
perMark.note: The circle is perfectly centered on the distinctive W-shaped eye.

perMark.label: Fin runs the whole body
perMark.note: The circle is extremely large and overlaps the entire body instead of highlighting the narrow outer fin.

perMark.label: Broad mottled body
perMark.note: The circle is oversized and shifted too far down, extending off the animal into the sandy background.

validation.summary: The eye mark is well-placed, but circles 2 and 3 are far too large and overlap excessively. Shrink and reposition circle 2 to target the narrow outer fin edge, and adjust circle 3 to focus cleanly on the broad mottled mantle.

marks.label: Small ear-like fins
marks.description: Two small, rounded ear-like fins near the head (not running down the body) mark out the little bobtail.

marks.label: Stubby body, iridescent specks
marks.description: A tiny (about 2 cm), stubby rounded body flecked with iridescent specks. Buries in clean sand by day with only the eyes showing.

perMark.label: Small ear-like fins
perMark.note: The small rounded fin is visible inside the circle, though it is slightly out of focus.

perMark.label: Stubby body, iridescent specks
perMark.note: The circle perfectly highlights the stubby body covered in brilliant iridescent specks and dark chromatophores.

validation.summary: The annotations are well-placed and clearly highlight the key diagnostic features of the Atlantic Bobtail squid.

marks.label: Extremely long, thin body
marks.description: An exceptionally slender, elongated body tapering to a long pointed snout — quite unlike any other small UK fish.

marks.label: Long pointed snout
marks.description: A long, fine, tweezer-like snout for picking tiny crustaceans out of weed.

marks.label: Row of short spines before dorsal
marks.description: A row of about fifteen tiny separate spines runs along the back in front of the dorsal fin (hence the name); a slim tail.

perMark.label: Extremely long, thin body
perMark.note: The circle is extremely large and encompasses most of the body, but it is off-center and could be sized better to show the overall silhouette.

perMark.label: Long pointed snout
perMark.note: The circle is perfectly centered on the long, tweezer-like snout.

perMark.label: Row of short spines before dorsal
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the mid-body and does not clearly show the tiny dorsal spines, which are very difficult to resolve in this lighting.

validation.summary: The snout is well-marked, but circle 1 is unnecessarily large and circle 3 is placed too far back on the body where the spines are not visible. Adjust circle 1 to frame the entire body shape and move circle 3 forward along the back where the spines should be.

marks.label: Keel of spiny scutes on belly
marks.description: A row of small, sharp scutes runs along the belly keel. Run a finger along it and you feel them. The most reliable sprat tell.

marks.label: No dark spot behind gill
marks.description: Unlike the herring, the sprat has no dark spot at the shoulder. A small (to ~15 cm), slim, bright-silver baitfish.

marks.label: Single small dorsal fin
marks.description: One short dorsal fin over the middle of the back, not forward of the pelvic fins as in the similar herring.

perMark.label: Keel of spiny scutes on belly
perMark.note: The circle is extremely large and encompasses most of the fish body and background rather than focusing on the belly keel.

perMark.label: No dark spot behind gill
perMark.note: The circle is well placed over the shoulder area behind the gill cover, showing a clean silver surface.

perMark.label: Single small dorsal fin
perMark.note: The circle correctly highlights the small dorsal fin on the back of the fish.

validation.summary: The circle for mark 1 is far too large and needs to be resized and shifted to target only the spiny belly keel. Marks 2 and 3 are well placed.

marks.label: Squat rounded whorl
marks.description: A squat, bluntly conical shell with purple zigzag streaks - flatter and rounder than the pointed painted top shell.

marks.label: Open navel hole underneath
marks.description: The giveaway: a distinct open navel (umbilicus) hole at the centre of the underside. The painted top shell has none.

marks.label: Squat rounded shell
marks.description: A low, rounded top-shell whorl, not a tall pointed spire.

marks.label: Reddish-purple streaks
marks.description: Diagonal reddish-purple streaks over a grey-green shell; an open navel-hole (umbilicus) sits at the base.

perMark.label: Squat rounded whorl
perMark.note: The circle for mark 1 is completely missing from the image.

perMark.label: Open navel hole underneath
perMark.note: The circle is placed in the far top-left corner on the background rock, and the underside of the shell is not visible anyway.

perMark.label: Squat rounded shell
perMark.note: The large circle correctly encompasses the low, rounded shell of the top shell.

perMark.label: Reddish-purple streaks
perMark.note: The circle is well-placed on the dark diagonal streaks of the shell, though they appear more greenish-black than reddish-purple here.

validation.summary: Mark 1 is missing entirely and mark 2 is misaligned in the top-left corner on background rock. Additionally, the diagnostic open navel hole is on the underside and cannot be seen in this top-down view, so mark 2 should be removed or the photo replaced with one showing the underside.

marks.label: Blue-green lines on the face
marks.description: A maze of blue-green wavy lines over the cheek and gill cover, vivid on breeding males.

marks.label: Dark spot on the tail base
marks.description: A small dark spot in the centre of the tail base, present in both sexes; the most reliable corkwing tell.

marks.label: Comma behind the eye
marks.description: A short dark comma-shaped mark just behind the eye (clearest on males).

perMark.label: Blue-green lines on the face
perMark.note: The circle perfectly encompasses the vivid blue-green lines on the cheek and gill cover.

perMark.label: Dark spot on the tail base
perMark.note: The circle is perfectly centered on the distinct dark spot at the base of the tail.

perMark.label: Comma behind the eye
perMark.note: The circle is placed too high and far back, completely missing the comma mark behind the eye.

validation.summary: The photo is excellent and clearly shows all features, but circle 3 is misaligned. Reposition circle 3 to target the dark comma-shaped mark located directly behind the eye.

marks.label: Long cheek spine
marks.description: A long spine on the cheek (preopercular) reaches back past the gill opening, the tell vs the short-spined sea scorpion.

marks.label: Big spiny head, fan pectorals
marks.description: A broad, spiny, knobbly head and large fan-like pectoral fins; a stout, tapering body.

marks.label: Mottled camouflage
marks.description: Highly variable blotchy camouflage (green, brown, reddish), sitting motionless among weed and rock.

perMark.label: Long cheek spine
perMark.note: The circle is placed on the cheek area but is too small to clearly highlight the long spine extending past the gill cover.

perMark.label: Big spiny head, fan pectorals
perMark.note: The large circle correctly encompasses the spiny head and the base of the pectoral fins.

perMark.label: Mottled camouflage
perMark.note: The circle is extremely large and centered off the body, mostly highlighting the background rather than the mottled skin pattern.

validation.summary: The circles are poorly sized and aligned, with circle 3 almost entirely off the fish. Reposition circle 1 to clearly target the long preopercular spine and resize circle 3 to focus on the mottled skin pattern on the body.

marks.label: Bony scute ridge
marks.description: A row of hard, bony, keel-like scutes runs along the full length of the lateral line — unmistakable once you know to look.

marks.label: Two separate dorsal fins
marks.description: First dorsal is short and spiny; second is long and soft — two clearly separate fins unlike mackerel's continuous one.

marks.label: Lateral-line scutes
marks.description: A row of bony plates (scutes) along the lateral line - the key give-away of horse mackerel (a scad, family Carangidae).

marks.label: Large eye, blunt snout
marks.description: A notably large eye and blunt rounded head; metallic silver flanks without mackerel's wavy green bars.

marks.label: Large eye
marks.description: A noticeably large eye for the head - one of the first things you notice.

marks.label: Forked tail, slim body
marks.description: A slim, faintly striped silver body ending in a deeply forked tail.

perMark.label: Bony scute ridge
perMark.note: The circle is extremely large and covers almost the entire body of the fish rather than focusing on the ridge.

perMark.label: Two separate dorsal fins
perMark.note: The circle is placed too low and is too large, capturing the back of the fish rather than focusing on the dorsal fins.

perMark.label: Lateral-line scutes
perMark.note: The circle is tiny and placed far above the fish in the empty black background.

perMark.label: Large eye, blunt snout
perMark.note: The circle is well-centered on the head, showing both the large eye and the blunt snout clearly.

perMark.label: Large eye
perMark.note: The circle is perfectly centered on the large eye of the fish.

perMark.label: Forked tail, slim body
perMark.note: The circle is shifted too far to the left, mostly capturing empty water and only the tip of the tail.

validation.summary: Several circles are misaligned or poorly sized. Circle 3 is floating in the background, circle 1 is far too large, and circle 6 needs to be shifted right to properly cover the tail.

marks.label: Chin barbel
marks.description: A single whisker under the chin (shared with cod and poor cod; absent in pollack and saithe).

marks.label: Dark pectoral spot
marks.description: A dark blotch at the base of the pectoral fin - strongest in bib, and often the first thing you notice.

marks.label: Deep coppery body
marks.description: Much deeper-bodied than pollack or saithe, coppery with faint vertical bars - almost a bream-like profile.

perMark.label: Chin barbel
perMark.note: The single chin barbel is clearly visible inside the circle.

perMark.label: Dark pectoral spot
perMark.note: The dark spot at the base of the pectoral fin is perfectly centered and highly visible.

perMark.label: Deep coppery body
perMark.note: The large circle captures the deep body shape and the distinct vertical banding pattern.

validation.summary: The photo is of excellent quality and all three diagnostic features are perfectly aligned and clearly visible.

marks.label: Chin barbel
marks.description: A short whisker under the chin, like bib's. Poor cod is the smaller, slimmer of the two.

marks.label: Large eye
marks.description: A noticeably large eye for the size of the head - poor cod is a small (to ~15 cm), big-eyed gadoid.

marks.label: Slimmer body than bib
marks.description: Coppery but more slender and tapering than the deep-bodied bib; three dorsal fins.

perMark.label: Chin barbel
perMark.note: The circle is placed slightly too high, overlapping the eye instead of centering on the chin barbel below.

perMark.label: Large eye
perMark.note: The circle is placed too low on the chin area rather than centering on the large eye.

perMark.label: Slimmer body than bib
perMark.note: The large circle correctly encompasses the slender, tapering body shape of the fish.

validation.summary: The labels for 1 and 2 are swapped or misaligned. Swap the positions of circle 1 and circle 2 so that circle 2 aligns with the eye and circle 1 aligns with the chin barbel.
